author: @glencahalin4786
text: Taking into account your preference for ease of making ( and minimal extra bits that add complication/points of failure/maintenance ) I've revised my previous idea, of dual concentric cones, to just make the thickness of the single cone, so that, at their junction,  it's outer diameter matches the outer diameter of the turbine, giving smoother air flow there and ( hopefully ) a small increase in efficiency.

G'Day,,have been watching this new design with great interest,,just to chuck my 2cents worth,,I agree with making the height smaller,,but suggest you makes them wider and apply more curve to the Blades,,Directing the exhaust to make more rotation,,or more power to the rotation,

Edit;think what if you had your turbine,with a venturi affect under it,generate your own low pressure zone under your turbine,,this should increase the velocity of the air,going through your turbine,,but that sorta stuff your design from any direction thing going for it,,
wonder how you would go with them stacked to-gether,,like a steam turbine use all the heat and expansion making max use of the energy,,so a slightly bigger rotor under neath,That would depend on how fast we can accelorate the flow going through it,using your cone design,
but Love it,,

author: @salimufari
text: So CalTech made a VAWT that was about 50 feet tall & had a shroud over the windward blade path to also compress the leeward blade wind entry. This gave nearly double the output relative to the wind speed as it blocked the return stroke drag. Your image & mention of the 'oldest VAWT' in Iran absolutely validated an idea I've been working on. Fingers crossed I can make it work.

author: @elvistesla3179
text: I keep thinking it is all about getting as much wind through your arch form as possible and finding the optimal combination to harvest the kinetic energy of it.  I don't think any one form will optimize and I think arrays or comninations of arrays (IE utilizing diffused and/or concentrated wind a second time in the process).  There is only so much you can concentrate so what do you do if you have leftover wind?  Diffuse and re-use?  It feels like it is all all about getting as much wind volume through in the most efficient way (vorex) that ALSO lets you harvest and transmute the most energy in the process .........without causing damage level stress and wear on your arch form (s).   I don't know that me stating the obvious is helpful to you - but it sometimes is to me.  LOL   I like to really define my goals in this manner.  What am I putting in, and what do I want out?  the most extreme version of capturing all the wind kinetic energy is a flat wall facing it.  Then you work your way down from there.   When I shoot my bow, in my mind I say "be the arrow".  When I troubleshoot an IP network I say to myself "be the IP packet".  When I am harnessing wind I say " be the wind".  Now how do I get through whatever contraption you made "being the wind" and what happens to me in the process?

replies.author: @elvistesla3179
replies.text: PS....once you figure the above out....then you have to get the most efficient transmutation of kinetic energy into electrical current.    Then the final hurdle......how do I cost effectively store and retrieve all this harness worthy energy?

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Good systems thinking.
I classify outcomes as follows:
a. Expected & desired (designed for)
b. Expected & not desired. (not designed for)
c. Unexpected & desired (an unexpected advantage - this video is an example of a feature derived through adding an element to the existing design)
d. Unexpected & not desired.(Difficult to design our as it's unexpected - hopefully feature is detected prior to production/use)

author: @glencahalin4786
text: For the Waters Turbine I'd think a dual concentric cone would give a further improvement in efficiency. The centre cone used as you have but a ( only slightly ) bigger cone around the outside. The "working air" , which is then coming out perpendicular to the air going in is slow and slowing the "working air" so the outer cone could thereby be used to direct the air, used to turn it,  away from the outer rim of the rotator in the direction of the prevailing air stream. Which should also have the effect of lowering the air pressure in that vicinity making the "working air" exit faster at the low air speeds and giving it a lower air speed start up.

author: @danedmiston9673
text: "Super Efficient", "Huge Improvement", "Betz Beater", and other big claims are great at getting attention.  The scientific method includes transparent, verifiable, and repeatable test results.  Independent third party test results (positive or negative) are hard to find but provide the most useful information.

author: @tommycrist1856
text: a cone/funnel compresses the air naturally and that is what you need for any wind powered generator. I'm using one on my Darwin/Telsa hybrid. worked a little too well, so the size of the intake cone is important as is the outflow opening.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: Other simple improvements include a ducted vertical turbine, a vertical fin and rotating basis that changes the direction of the HAWT according to the wind, join two HAWTs into a single contra-rotating one (this doesn't require a gearbox if you use one generator per blade set), ...

replies.author: @tommycrist1856
replies.text: @vitordelimaThanks for the reply. My problem isn't wind direction. I used an 8-sided darwin wind catcher sitting on top of a funnel feeding a tesla turbine. When the turbine gets to speed it starts sucking in more air. more air, more rpms. more rpms, more air. A vicious loop. I think I can limit that by making the outflow hole adjustable. But that is defeating my idea of a generator that is a place and forget model.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @tommycrist1856 Maybe there is a static nozzle that will at most output a certain airflow velocity and pressure that is supported by your turbine.

replies.author: @tommycrist1856
replies.text: @vitordelima Thanks for replying. I've tried different size nozzles, and what I have now is just a slit about the size of a thick sheet of paper.  Because I'm using air bearings there isn't any friction, so once it starts spinning, it keeps spinning. I built and tried one the other day and it caught fire, even with a full load on it. That is why I'm doing a 4 to 1 ratio with my discs. Smaller discs in the middle, larger discs on the ends of my shaft. smaller discs and out flow should do the trick.

replies.author: @EarthPlusPlastic
replies.text: @tommycrist1856  Would you be able to get any use out adding a flywheel to the setup?  I would assume that the flywheel would act as a limiter to the top speed but still retain a great deal of the energy.  You could try different sizes and/or weights.  Also, as I believe Rob pointed out in a earlier video, the flywheel would "smooth" out the output.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: I suspect if you make the energy measurements with the cone (or hyperbola) and use that as the collection diameter, you'll have better agreement with the Betz limit.  The "exhaust" air out of your Waters turbine will be blown downstream by air coming around the cone face, which is closer to the assumptions implicit in the limit derivation.
